title: Read the Bills Act
stage_label: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Rules and Administration.
plain_summary: The Read the Bills Act would make new rules for how Congress handles bills. Every bill would need to explain which part of the Constitution gives Congress the power to make that law, and bills that change existing laws must show exactly what they are changing. Before Congress can vote on any bill, it must be posted online for seven days, read out loud to all members, and each member who votes yes must promise in writing that they either listened to the whole reading or read the entire bill themselves. If these rules are broken, people can sue to stop the bill from becoming law.
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Rules and Administration.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Read the Bills Act.

sections.title: Section 2 Constitutional Authority Statement
sections.text: This law is created using powers that the Constitution gives to Congress. The Constitution allows each house of Congress to make its own rules and keep records of what it does. The Constitution also says that bills must actually be passed by both the House and Senate to become law, not just moved through them quickly. Congress can make laws that help it follow its own rules. People who are hurt when laws are passed without following proper rules can take their case to court. This right to go to court comes from the part of the Constitution that sets up the court system.

sections.title: Section 3 Findings
sections.text: Congress has found these facts. The Constitution gives all lawmaking power to Congress. Each member of Congress is elected by the people and must represent them when making laws. The Constitution limits Congress to only making laws it is allowed to make. Each member of Congress must take an oath to support the Constitution and only make laws that follow it. To make sure Congress is accountable to the people, the Constitution requires each house of Congress to keep records and publish them. Only bills that have passed both the House and Senate can become law if the President does not veto them. Thomas Jefferson wrote in eighteen oh one that following proper rules protects the minority from the majority and prevents those in power from having too much control. Jefferson said the Senate used to read every bill three times, with two full readings by the clerk and a third reading of just the title because every Senator had a printed copy. The House used to read bills twice in full by the clerk and once more completely by the Speaker before voting. Today the Senate only reads the title of bills unless it decides otherwise. The House is supposed to read bills in full but often skips this by agreement and only reads titles before voting, even when members do not have printed copies. Current law says members must have written copies of bills before voting, but Congress can vote on bills without written copies during the last six days of a session. Because Congress stopped reading bills in full and allowed some bills to be voted on without printed copies, several problems have happened. Congress has passed very long bills written mostly by unelected government workers that are hard to understand and contain hidden benefits for special interests. The people and their elected representatives do not get a fair chance to examine bills and amendments before they are voted on. People have lost confidence in Congress because they do not get enough notice before votes happen. The integrity of the lawmaking process is questioned because senators and representatives may vote without listening to the full reading or reading the bills themselves. Current federal law already sets rules about how bills must be written and how laws are made, including the required words that start all laws and resolutions, how sections must be numbered, and how bills are handled after they pass.

sections.title: Section 4 Text of Bill or Resolution to Specify Its Constitutional Authority, Current Law
sections.text: This section adds new rules to federal law. Every bill or resolution introduced in either house of Congress must include a statement that cites the specific parts of the Constitution that give Congress the power to pass that bill or resolution. If a bill or resolution does not include this statement, the House Clerk or Senate Secretary cannot accept it. This rule also applies to any bill or resolution brought to the floor for consideration, including those from committees, conference committees, or offered as amendments by managers. Bills without this constitutional authority statement cannot be voted on for final passage. Neither house of Congress can waive or change this requirement by any vote, resolution, or agreement. Every bill or resolution that would change existing law must show the current version of the entire section of law it wants to change, word for word. It must show the proposed changes and how the law would read with those changes, unless the bill would strike an entire section. If a bill does not meet these requirements, the House Clerk or Senate Secretary cannot accept it. This rule applies to all bills brought to the floor, including those from committees, conference committees, or manager amendments. Bills that do not meet these requirements cannot be voted on for final passage. Congress cannot waive or change this requirement. Before any final vote on a bill (except private bills) or resolution, several things must happen. The full text must be published at least seven days before the vote on the official websites of both houses of Congress in a format that is easy for the public to find, search, and download. Public notice of the specific week when the vote will happen must be posted at least six days before Monday of that week. If the vote does not happen that week, a new notice is required. The House Clerk or Senate Secretary must read the full text of the bill or resolution out loud, word for word, to their house of Congress. A quorum (minimum number of members required to do business) must be present and listening during the entire reading. If a bill has already passed one house, it does not need to be read again in that house, but any amendments must be read in full to both houses. Before voting for final passage, each Senator and House member must sign a sworn statement saying they either listened to the entire reading or read the entire bill themselves before voting. Members who vote against the bill do not need to sign this statement. The Secretary of the Senate and House Clerk must keep copies of these sworn statements. Each house must record in its official journal that all the publishing, notice, reading, and sworn statement requirements were met for each vote. Congress cannot waive or change these requirements by any vote, resolution, or agreement. Any law passed without following these rules has no legal effect and cannot be enforced in any way. People can sue the federal government in court if they are harmed by enforcement of laws that were not passed according to these rules. Members of Congress can sue if their house fails to follow these rules. Citizens can sue if their Senator or Representative fails to meet their obligations under these rules.

sections.title: Section 5 Technical and Conforming Amendments
sections.text: The table of contents for this part of federal law is updated to include the four new sections that were added.

sections.title: Section 6 Severability Clause
sections.text: If any court finds that any part of this law is invalid, the rest of the law will still be in effect and enforceable.
